3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Weinstein, S., Bashir, A., Lee, S., Al-shoaibi, A. A. A., Shao, I. Y., Ganson, K. T., Testa, A., He, J., & Garber, A. K. (2024). Associations of contemporary screen time modalities with early adolescent nutrition. Academic Pediatrics, 24, 748-754.

3C_Exposure.how_operationalized: Self-reported hours per day for each modality, weekday and weekend separately; weighted average ([weekday x 5] + [weekend x 2])/7; reported as continuous and categorical (0-2, >2-4, >4-6, >6 hrs/day)

3D_Outcome.measure_used: Mediterranean-DASH Intervention for Neurodegenerative Delay (MIND) diet score, derived from Child Nutrition Assessment (14-item Food Frequency Questionnaire, parent-reported); sum score of protective and neurodegenerative food groups (max 14)
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: N/A - continuous; mean MIND diet score = 8.00 (SD 2.45)

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: 16 separate regression models: screen time at baseline (Year 0) predicting MIND diet at Year 1 (prospective), and screen time at Year 1 predicting MIND diet at Year 1 (cross-sectional), for total screen time and each of 6 modalities. Additional model: baseline screen time + change in screen time predicting MIND diet. Propensity weights applied.
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: Race/ethnicity, sex, age, household income, parent education, depression (CBCL), parent monitoring, study site
3E_Design_Analysis.SES_in_model: yes - household income and parent education included
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Multivariable linear regressions were used to investigate the associations between screen time use, total screen time (continuous and categorical) and each of the 6 modalities (continuous), at baseline (year 0) and 1-year follow-up (year 1), and adherence to the MIND diet at the 1-year follow-up (year 1) for a total of 16 individual regression models.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Total screen time (baseline, hrs/day) -> MIND diet score (Year 1), prospective
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Each additional hour per day of total screen time at both year 0 (-0.07, 95% CI: -0.09, -0.05) and year 1 (-0.07, 95% CI: -0.09, -0.05) was associated with a lower nutrition score at year 1.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Videos/YouTube (baseline, hrs/day) -> MIND diet score (Year 1), prospective
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Each additional hour per day of screen time at year 0 and year 1 in the form of watching videos ([-0.18, 95% CI:-0.24, -0.12]; [-0.24, 95% CI:-0.30, -0.19])

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Video games (baseline, hrs/day) -> MIND diet score (Year 1), prospective
Television (baseline, hrs/day) -> MIND diet score (Year 1), prospective
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: and television ([-0.14, 95% CI:-0.20, -0.07]; [-0.07, 95% CI:-0.14, -0.01]) was associated with a lower nutrition score at year 1.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Social media (Year 1, cross-sectional) -> MIND diet score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: social media (-0.16, 95% CI:-0.28, -0.05) screen use at year 1 were associated with lower nutrition scores, cross-sectionally.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: >2-4 hrs/day total screen time (baseline) vs 0-2 hrs -> MIND diet score
>6 hrs/day total screen time (baseline) vs 0-2 hrs -> MIND diet score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: >6 (-0.65, 95% CI: -0.84, -0.45) hours per day of total screen time were associated with lower MIND diet scores

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Change in total screen time (baseline to Year 1) -> MIND diet score, controlling for baseline screen time
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Increases in total screen time from baseline to year 1 were associated with lower MIND diet scores, even after accounting for baseline total screen time... change in total screen time B = -0.05 (-0.08, -0.03)

3G_Interpretation.verbatim_recommendation: Clinicians should assess for nutritional status at each pediatric health assessment visit, which includes a nutritional history and assessment of food intake and behavior. Clinicians should also consider assessing for screen time and provide resources for parents such as a Family Media Use plan, which could involve screen-free times during family meals to encourage communication within families and to avoid eating while distracted in front of screens.

3H_Rhetorical.pattern_detail: Authors acknowledge 'The effect sizes observed in our study were relatively small; however, the decrements in diet quality were expressed relative to 1 hour increase in screen time, whereas total screen time was 3.80 at baseline and 4.61 h at year 1.' They then proceed to recommend clinicians assess screen time and implement Family Media Use plans. The per-hour B of -0.07 on a 14-point diet scale is very small.

3I_Flags.ambiguity_notes: Effect sizes are very small: -0.07 MIND diet points per additional hour of total screen time on a 14-point scale. Authors acknowledge small effects but frame them as clinically relevant via accumulation argument. Nutrition outcome (parent-reported food frequency) may have measurement limitations. Social media and texting were only significant cross-sectionally, not prospectively.
